text: Transrectal natural orifice specimen extraction, a safe and feasible technique in patients undergoing subtotal colectomy. Traditional laparoscopic subtotal colectomy requires a mini-laparotomy or larger than usual ileostomy incision for specimen extraction, which contributes to increased post-operative pain and long-term risk of hernia formation. Transrectal specimen extraction has the potential to reduce this morbidity and improve surgical outcomes.
We have performed TR nose in several patients during the last year. This video aims to demonstrate the six steps for TR nose as developed in our institution.
The surgeon completes the laparoscopic subtotal colonic mobilization, followed by the transection of the specimen at proximal and distal points.

The surgeon inserts washout tubing into the apex of the rectal stump to perform a rectal washout, ensuring thorough cleansing of the stump to minimize contamination of the pelvic cavity. The rectal stump is then opened.

The surgeon inserts a nylon tape via the perineum into the pelvis, followed by a protective plastic sheath, creating a smooth surface for the specimen to slide. The specimen is then inserted into the rectal stump and extracted through the rectum using a combination of pushing from above and pulling from below by a second operator.

The surgeon encounters difficulty extracting the specimen due to gaseous acetyl distention. An incision is made in the partially extracted specimen near the anal canal, allowing the insertion of a flatus tube to deflate the specimen, facilitating complete extraction.
The surgeon cross-staples the rectal stump and performs an ileostomy, completing the final steps of the procedure.

In a challenging case, the distal specimen is extracted easily, but the bulky transverse colon complicates the process. The surgeon makes several incisions into the colon and performs sequential flatus tube decompressions. A bulky omentum is identified and dissected off the specimen, allowing the extraction to continue.
The surgeon addresses gaseous cecal distention using a standard technique, allowing for the successful completion of specimen extraction.
In conclusion, a stepwise approach to transrectal specimen extraction is feasible and it may be applied to patients having a laparoscopic subtotal colectomy. Thank you for watching this video.
